coredata.dc:title: Glucose oxidase@Cu-hemin metal-organic framework for colorimetric analysis of glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Chunhua
Du, Yue
Wang, Shiqi
Wang, Li
Song, Yonghai
coredata.dc:description: The work presents a novel glucose oxidase@Cu-hemin metal-organic frameworks (GOD@ Cu-hemin MOFs) with a ball-flower structure as bienzymatic catalysts for detection of glucose. The GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs exhibits great stability as compared with free horseradish peroxidase and GOD toward harsh conditions because the ball-flower-like shell of Cu-hemin MOF effectively protects from GOD. Thus, the GOD@Cu-Hemin MOFs can be used in external harsh conditions such as high temperature and acid/base. The GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs is capable of sensitive and selective detection of glucose via peroxidase-like of Cu-hemin MOFs and GOD by using 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a substrate. Under the existence of glucose, O2 is reduced into H2O2 via GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs. The produced H2O2 as well as Cu-hemin MOFs oxidize TMB into blue oxTMB which shows UV–Vis absorbance at 652. The absorption intensity of oxTMB linearly increases with the increasing concentration of glucose from 0.01 to 1.0 mM with detection limit of 2.8 μM. An integrated agarose hydrogel film (Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB) sensor is rationally designed for colorimetric detection of glucose. The sensor displays a response range of 30 μM-0.8 mM with a detection limit of 0.01 mM. The result indicates that the Cu-hemin MOFs are an ideal carrier for the encapsulation of enzymes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic frameworks
Agarose hydrogel film
Glucose oxidase
Hemin
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of integrated agarose-based hydrogel film (Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB)
Kinetic analysis of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF as bienzymatic catalysts
Characterization of Cu-hemin MOF and GOD@Cu-hemin MOF

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21465015 and 21665012 ), and the Open Project Program of Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, Jiangxi Normal University (No. KLFS-KF-201919 ; KLFS-KF-201913 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrations of the peroxidase-like activity of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF and visualization of glucose levels using Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB hydrogel films as the integrated colorimetric platform.
SEM images of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF. (Inset: the size distribution histogram of GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs).

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of (black) GOD, (red) Cu-hemin MOF and (green) GOD@Cu-hemin MOF. (B) FT-IR spectra of (black) Cu-hemin MOF and (red) GOD@Cu-hemin MOF. (C) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of (black) Cu-hemin MOF and (red) GOD@Cu-hemin MOF. Y-axis values are enhanced to overcome its overlap with the other isotherm. (D) XRD of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF and Cu-hemin MOF. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of TMB (0.8 mM) reaction solutions catalytically oxidized by (black) no catalyst, (red) hemin (0.2 mg mL−1), (blue) GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs (0.2 mg mL−1) and (green) Cu-hemin MOFs (0.2 mg mL−1) in the presence of 0.6 mM H2O2. (B) Effects of different pH on the absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Absorption spectra of the GOD@Cu-hemin MOF system in the presence of different glucose concentrations (0.01 mM, 0.02 mM, 0.04 mM, 0.06 mM, 0.08 mM, 0.1 mM, 0.2 mM, 0.4 mM, 0.6 mM, 0.8 mM, 1.0 mM, 2.0 mM, 3.0 mM, 4.0 mM). The inset shows the corresponding photographs under different concentrations of glucose. (B) Linear calibration curve of the absorbance at 652 nm against glucose concentration.

(A) Absorption spectra of the Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB system in the presence of different glucose concentrations (0.0 mM, 0.05 mM, 0.1 mM, 0.4 mM, 0.6 mM and 0.8 mM). (B) Linear calibration curve of the absorbance at 652 nm against glucose concentration. The inset shows the corresponding hydrogel film (Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB) for different concentrations of glucose.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of (black) GOD+HRP and (red) GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs at different pH. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of (black) GOD+HRP and (red) GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs at different temperature. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of (red) GOD+HRP and (black) GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs at several days. (D) Selectivity test of the GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs system against potential interfering substances. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Glucose oxidase@Cu-hemin metal-organic framework for colorimetric analysis of glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology, Jiangxi Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Road, Nanchang 330022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology, Jiangxi Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Road, Nanchang 330022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The work presents a novel glucose oxidase@Cu-hemin metal-organic frameworks (GOD@ Cu-hemin MOFs) with a ball-flower structure as bienzymatic catalysts for detection of glucose. The GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs exhibits great stability as compared with free horseradish peroxidase and GOD toward harsh conditions because the ball-flower-like shell of Cu-hemin MOF effectively protects from GOD. Thus, the GOD@Cu-Hemin MOFs can be used in external harsh conditions such as high temperature and acid/base. The GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs is capable of sensitive and selective detection of glucose via peroxidase-like of Cu-hemin MOFs and GOD by using 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a substrate. Under the existence of glucose, O2 is reduced into H2O2 via GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs. The produced H2O2 as well as Cu-hemin MOFs oxidize TMB into blue oxTMB which shows UV–Vis absorbance at 652. The absorption intensity of oxTMB linearly increases with the increasing concentration of glucose from 0.01 to 1.0 mM with detection limit of 2.8 μM. An integrated agarose hydrogel film (Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB) sensor is rationally designed for colorimetric detection of glucose. The sensor displays a response range of 30 μM-0.8 mM with a detection limit of 0.01 mM. The result indicates that the Cu-hemin MOFs are an ideal carrier for the encapsulation of enzymes.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes has become one of the main diseases in today's world, which is leading many scientists to devote their efforts to developing glucose detection equipments [1–3]. With the efforts of many scientists, many glucose detection equipments are developed. Portable glucose meters have also been commercialized [4–8]. Commercial portable glucose meters and most reported glucose detection devices are based on glucose oxidase (GOD). The use of devices is limited by GOD′ fragile nature, such as narrow temperature ranges, higher requirements for the pH environment, insupportableness for many metal ions and most organic solvents, etc. [9–13]. Although some enzyme-free glucose detection devices are also developed, they generally need to be used in alkaline and cannot be directly used like commercial glucose meters, which are also inconvenient. In addition, the selectivity of non-enzymatic detection equipments is generally poor [14–18]. Therefore, the glucose detection equipments based on GOD are still the main research direction, which requires researchers to develop stable GOD compounds for constructing glucose detection devices that can be used under harsh conditions [19–23].

Biomineralization is a good method to prepare bioenzyme composites which can improve bioenzyme's stability and their application [24]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) established from the coordination of metal ions with organic bridge ligands are attractive hybrid materials [25–29]. MOFs have large surface area and adjustable porous structure which has been extensively applied in various fields, such as energy storage, sensing, catalysis, drug delivery and separation, etc. [30–34]. In particular, the MOFs as biomolecule carrier is attracting more and more interest [35–39]. MOFs can protect the enzymes from the external harsh conditions [40–44]. Therefore, the immobilizing enzymes in MOFs can greatly enhance their stability and catalytic activity [45–49]. As we all know, the catalytic activity of natural enzymes is sensitive to external environments [47]. Therefore, it is indispensable to prepare MOFs by using biocompatible building blocks as the host of enzyme under mild conditions in order to better maintain the catalytic activity of the immobilized enzymes [48].

Inspired by a unique Cu-hemin MOF with peroxidase-like activity [50], a facile and efficient biomimetic mineralization strategy is proposed to fabricate GOD@Cu-hemin MOF which both has the biological activity of GOD and horseradish peroxidase (HRP). The GOD@Cu-hemin MOF with ball-flower structure as enzymatic catalysts not only exhibits remarkable enzyme's activity, but also gains excellent stability toward harsh conditions. The GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs can be used to sensitive and selective detection of glucose by using 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a chromogenic substrate. Under the existence of glucose, O2 is reduced into H2O2 via GOD@Cu-hemin MOFs. The produced H2O2 as well as Cu-hemin MOFs oxidize TMB into blue oxTMB. An integrated agarose-based hydrogel film (Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB) sensor is rationally designed for colorimetric detection of glucose which is more convenient as compared other developed sensors. The GOD@Cu-Hemin MOFs can be stored in external harsh conditions such as high temperature and accordingly the glucose sensor based on GOD@Cu-Hemin MOFs is convenient for users. The result indicates that the Cu-hemin MOFs is an ideal carrier for the encapsulation of enzymes.

GOD and hemin were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, TMB, H2O2, glucose, uric acid, galactose, fructose, L-cysteine, mannose, sucrose, chitosan, cysteine, glutathione, urea, Na2HPO4·12H2O, NaH2PO4·2H2O, acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate (NaAc), etc. were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The NaAc-HAc buffer solution was prepared by acetic acid and sodium acetate, and the phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was prepared by using Na2HPO4·12H2O and NaH2PO4·2H2O. All solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water purified by a Millipore-Q system (18.2 MΩ cm−1).

UV–vis absorbance spectra were performed on a Hitachi U-3900H spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) spectrometer (Nicolet, USA) was applied to record FT-IR spectra. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) were taken by HITACHI S-3400 N SEM with a Phoenix energy X-ray analyzer. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was performed on a D/Max 2500 V/PC X-ray powder diffractometer using Cu Ka radiation (k = 0.154056 nm, 40 kV, 200 mA). The N2 adsorption/desorption data were collected at 77 K using an ASAP 2020 instrument (Micromeritics).

10 mg GOD were added into a aqueous solution of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (43 mM, 2 mL). A solution of hemin (0.50 mM, 1 mL) (pH = 7) was also prepared by dissolving it in PBS [24,50]. Two solutions were mixed and violently stirred for 10 s. The obtained mixed solution was aged for 12 h to generate yellow-green precipitate at room temperature. The obtained precipitate was acquired by centrifugation at 5000 r.m.p. about 5 min. Then, the obtained precipitate was washed with ultra-pure water and centrifuged. Finally, the obtained products were dried in the freeze-dryer for 24 h. In the absence of GOD, the Cu-hemin MOF was also prepared based on the same procedures. The preparation procedures were shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of integrated agarose-based hydrogel film (Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB)
ce:section.ce:para: Agarose hydrogels of 2.5% (w/v) were prepared by dissolving 0.25 g agarose in 10 mL ultra-pure water. It was heated in a water bath until agarose was completely dissolved. After stopping heating and waiting for a few minutes, 50 μL of 2 mg mL−1 GOD@Cu-hemin MOF and 50 μL of 8 mM TMB solution were added into the above solution. The mixture was stirred with a glass rod until homogeneous mixing. The agarose mixture was finally poured into the petri dish to prepare a round hydrogel film with a diameter of 1 cm after cooling to room temperature as shown in Fig. 1.

For the measurement of glucose by using UV–Vis spectroscopy, 50 μL GOD@Cu-hemin MOF suspension (2.0 mg mL−1), 16 μL of TMB (25 mM) and 50 μL standard glucose solution with different concentration of glucose were mixed and the final volume controlled to 500 μL with PBS solution. After incubation 20 min at 45 °C, the UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded at 652 nm. When the real sample was determined, the serum samples replaced the glucose standard solution. For the measurement of glucose by using Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB, 5.0 μL standard glucose solution with different concentration of glucose were mixed with 45 μL PBS solution. Then the mixed solution was dropped on Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB. After incubation 20 min, the Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic analysis of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF as bienzymatic catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF was firstly analyzed by measuring the steady-state kinetic parameters for GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB/H2O2. The concentration of oxidized TMB (oxTMB) was determined by its absorbance at 652 nm with a molar extinction coefficient of 3.9 × 104 M−1 cm−1. With the Lineweaver-Burk plot of 1/v = Km/Vmax(1/[S] + 1/Km), the Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF was calculated, where v represents the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, S corresponds to the concentration of substrate, and Km is the Michaelis constant. As shown in Fig. S1A (Supporting Information), in GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB/H2O2 system, other conditions were kept unchanged except the amount of TMB. Km = 0.43 mM and Vmax = 7.12 × 10−8 M s−1 of TMB was obtained. Other conditions were kept unchanged but change the amount of H2O2. As shown in Fig. S1B (Supporting Information), Km = 1.30 mM and Vmax = 4.19 × 10−8 M s−1 of H2O2 were obtained. According to the comparison in Table S1 (Supporting Information), the catalytic effect of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF nanocomposite is better than that of HRP but the maximum reaction rate is not high than HRP [51], indicating that the catalytic ability of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF nanocomposite was enough for the following experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Cu-hemin MOF and GOD@Cu-hemin MOF
ce:section.ce:para: The GOD@Cu-hemin MOF is formed by the self-assembly of GOD, Cu2+ and hemin under mild conditions though biomimetic mineralization. The morphology of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF is explored by SEM. Fig. 2A shows that GOD@Cu-hemin MOF chiefly exists in single ball-flower structure with a rough surface. The diameter of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF ranges from 6.75 to 7.75 μm (Inset). Furthermore, there are lots of large pores with different size in the GOD@Cu-hemin MOF, which is benefit for the mass transfer. Fig. 2B displays high-resolution SEM image of broken GOD@Cu-hemin MOF, which indicates that the GOD@Cu-hemin MOF is the solid sphere. SEM images of Cu-hemin MOF are shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information), and the size and morphology are similar to that of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF. The EDS of Cu-hemin MOF (Fig. S3A, Supporting Information) exhibits C, N, O and Cu elements and GOD@Cu-hemin MOF (Fig. S3B, Supporting Information) exhibits C, N, O, Cu and P elements. The appearance of P element is because GOD are encapsulated into Cu-hemin MOF with biomimetic mineralization, indicating that GOD@Cu-hemin MOF has been successfully prepared.

The characterizations of GOD, Cu-hemin MOF and GOD@Cu-hemin MOF nanocomposites are further carried out to make sure the successful formation of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF. The UV–Vis absorption spectra show (Fig. 3A) that the Cu-hemin MOF has a weak adsorption peak at about 400 nm which is attributed to π-π* transition of the porphin ring. The GOD has three adsorption peaks at 279 nm, 396 nm and 646 nm. The intensive peak at 279 nm was attributed to the characteristic of polypeptide chains, and other two weak peaks at 396 and 646 nm were ascribed to the oxidized form of flavin group in protein structure [52]. The peaks also appear at the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF, indicating the GOD are successfully encapsulated into Cu-hemin MOF. Successful formation of Cu-hemin MOF and GOD@Cu-hemin MOF are also assessed by FT-IR (Fig. 3B). In FT-IR spectrum of Cu-hemin MOF, the CN, -CH3 and CN stretching bands in hemin appear at 1061 cm−1, 1384 cm−1 and 1683 cm−1, respectively [50]. The similar stretching bands also appear in the FT-IR spectrum of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF, like 1068 cm−1 of CN, 1384 cm−1 of -CH3 and 1658 cm−1 of CN. The peptide bond of -CO-NH- in protein at 3440 cm−1 and 1548 cm−1 evidently implies the existence of GOD. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the adsorption and desorption curves are inconsistent with hysteresis loops which indicates the existence of mesopores. The pore size distributions show the primary peaks being around 29.20 nm for Cu-hemin MOF and 25.53 nm for GOD@Cu-hemin MOF, respectively. The aperture of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF is smaller than that of Cu-hemin MOF, possibly because the encapsulation of GOD in Cu-hemin MOF causes the aperture to become smaller. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas are 27.13 m2 g−1 for Cu-hemin MOF and 50.06 m2 g−1 for GOD@Cu-hemin MOF, indicating that the specific surface area of the GOD@Cu-hemin MOF is larger than that of the Cu-hemin MOF. The XRD patterns of Cu-hemin MOF and GOD@Cu-hemin MOF show several peaks at 9.0°, 13.0°, 18.3°, 21.5°, 29.8°, 33.6°, 37.2°, 42.0°, 48.1°, 53.7° and 61.4°, implying that they are crystalline materials [47]. A peak around 10° is different for GOD@Cu-hemin MOF compared to the one without GOD. It may be ascribed to the array of GOD molecules in Cu-hemin MOF.

The glucose can be oxidized into gluconic acid by GOD in GOD@Cu-hemin MOF and simultaneously the GOD is reduced into a reduced GOD which reduces the dissolved O2 into H2O2. The produced H2O2 as well as peroxidase-like Cu-hemin MOF oxidize TMB into blue oxTMB which has a strong absorption peak at 652 nm [50]. In order to verify the feasibility of the speculation, several designs are carried out. As shown in Fig. 4A, no UV–Vis absorbance peak at 652 nm appears without catalyst. In the presence of hemin, apparent UV–Vis absorbance peak at 652 nm which is a typical absorption peak of oxTMB is observed, indicating good catalytic ability of hemin [33,34]. The peak before 400 nm can be ascribed to π-π* electron transitions in large conjugated systems. The peroxidase-like activity of hemin in Cu-hemin MOFs and GOD@Cu-hemin MOF is greatly enhanced. It may originate from the good dispersion of hemin and the synergistic effect. The hemin is easily aggregated into clusters when it is dissolved in water, while the coordination between hemin and Cu2+ make hemin have better dispersion in Cu-hemin MOFs and GOD@Cu-hemin MOF, which enhances the peroxidase-like activity of hemin greatly. Furthermore, the synergistic effect between hemin and Cu2+ also improves the peroxidase-like activity of hemin. The surrounding pH is also optimized, and the max UV–Vis absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm is at pH = 4–6 (Fig. 4B). At higher pH, the UV–Vis absorbance at 652 nm reduces gradually, which may result from the damage of Cu-hemin MOFs.

To obtain high performance of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF, the concentration of TMB and GOD@Cu-hemin MOF are investigated. As shown in Fig. S4A (Supporting Information), the UV–Vis absorbance intensity of oxTMB at 652 nm gradually increases with the increasing concentration of TMB in the presence of 0.6 mM glucose. When the concentration of TMB reaches 0.8 mM, the ultraviolet absorption intensity of oxTMB arrives at the maximum value. However, the UV–Vis absorbance intensity decreases following the further increasing concentration of TMB. The reason possibly is that the concentration of TMB is too high, leading to the aggregation of TMB. Therefore 0.8 mM TMB is used in the subsequent glucose detection experiments. The concentration of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF has a great influence on the ultraviolet absorption intensity. 0.10 M NaAc-HAc solution (pH = 6) mixed with different concentration of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF is used to investigate the effect of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF concentration. The UV–Vis absorbance intensity of oxTMB increases with increasing concentration of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF following 0.0–0.20 mg mL−1, indicating that the catalytic effect is enhanced. When the concentration is 0.20 mg mL−1, the ultraviolet absorption intensity reaches the maximal value and the UV absorption peak of oxTMB unchanges till GOD@Cu-hemin MOF reaches 0.320 mg mL−1 (Fig. S4B, Supporting Information). The result suggests all the TMB have been oxidized into oxTMB and accordingly the UV–Vis absorbance of oxTMB keeps constant. Therefore, the optimal concentration of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF is 0.20 mg mL−1.

Under the optimal conditions, the ultraviolet absorption intensity of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF-TMB significantly increases upon the addition of glucose. The result suggests that GOD@Cu-hemin MOF may be used for detecting glucose. As indicated in Fig. 5A, the absorption intensity of oxTMB progressively increases with the increasing concentration of glucose from 0.01 to 4.0 mM. Such an ultraviolet absorption intensity increasing can be clearly observed through eyes because the color gradually become blue (inset photo in Fig. 5A). A good linear correlation is also obtained between 10 μM–1.0 mM with a lower detection limit of 2.8 μM (Fig. 5B). The normal blood glucose concentration of healthy people is about 3.61–6.11 mM. So, we can dilute the blood sample ten times (see Section 2.5) and the blood glucose concentration is about 0.361–0.611 mM, which is suitable for measuring glucose by our method like most sensors [53–62]. As shown in Table S2 (Supporting Information) [53–62], the linear range of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF is wider than most other colorimetric methods apart from copper nanoclusters, but detection limit of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF is much lower than that of copper nanoclusters, which indicates that the as-prepared GOD@Cu-hemin MOF has certain practical value. The results are also superior to that of Hemin/GOD system (Fig. S5, Supporting Information) and that of GOD+Cu-hemin MOF (Fig. S6, Supporting Information).

The GOD@Cu-hemin MOF is prepared into Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB film. The obtained Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB film reveals an almost light-white color, manifesting few agglomeration and homogeneous distribution of TMB and GOD@Cu-hemin MOF within the hydrogel although both of them are naturally yellow-green, which provides a good surrounding to simply investigate the changing color. A good linear correlation is also obtained between Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB film at A652 nm and glucose concentration with 0.03–0.8 mM, and a detection limit of 0.01 mM (Fig. 6A). The color of the Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB film transforms from colorless to blue following the increasing glucose concentration as shown by inset photo in Fig. 6B.

The bioactivities of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF at different conditions are investigated and further compared to GOD-HRP. As compared with inorganic nanomaterials, all enzymes are easily deactivated under harsh conditions. For example, the bioactivity of HRP significantly declines under sub-optimal conditions. Fig. 7A shows that the ultraviolet absorption intensity of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF-TMB in NaAc-HAc solution (0.10 M) with the pH increasing from 3 to 6 is more stable than that of GOD-HRP-TMB. With the increasing of pH, the ultraviolet absorption intensity of both systems decreases because the alkaline condition affects the enzyme's activity sharply. But the GOD@Cu-hemin MOF-TMB is more stable than GOD-HRP-TMB at different pH. The reason may be the inorganic structure of Cu-hemin MOF can bear harsh pH surroundings. Besides, the special inorganic structure and the protective effect from ball-flower structure of Cu-hemin MOF probably can defend the activity of GOD at different pH [45]. After the GOD@Cu-hemin MOF composites are also soaked in NaAc-HAc buffer solution at the pH range from 3 to 8 for 2 h, the ball-flower structure is well remained (Fig. S7, Supporting Information). The result proves that the complexing ability between hemin, GOD and Cu2+ is strong enough to resist acid and base. The encapsulation of GOD in Cu-hemin MOF results in great enhancement of the acid/base-resistant ability due to the production from ball-flower structure of Cu-hemin MOF to GOD [46].

The effect of temperature is also discussed in Fig. 7B. The ultraviolet absorption intensity of GOD-HRP-TMB increases a bit as the temperature becomes from 20 °C to 25 °C, but it decreases with the increasing temperature from 25 °C to 60 °C. It possibly is because too high temperature makes the HRP and GOD to be deactivated. When the temperature reaches above 25 °C, the ultraviolet absorption intensity of oxTMB achieves the peak value, indicating the best surrounding temperature is 25 °C for GOD-HRP-TMB. The ultraviolet absorption intensity of GOD@Cu-hemin MOF-TMB is growing with the rising temperature from 20 °C to 45 °C. When the temperature is 45 °C, it achieves the maximum value. The phenomenon may be because the ball-flower structure of Cu-hemin MOF reserves the bioactivity of GOD. The Cu-hemin MOF is inorganic materials which's catalytic ability almost is not affected by temperature. About the descending ultraviolet absorption intensity from 45 °C to 60 °C, it may be because the temperature is so high that the structure of Cu-hemin MOF is damaged partially. Fig. 7C shows that the ultraviolet absorption intensity of GOD-HRP-TMB system decreases more violently as compared with GOD@Cu-hemin MOF-TMB with the increase of time. This phenomenon can be traced back to the biological inactivity of GOD and HRP. It proves again that the bioactivity of GOD is well protected by inorganic Cu-hemin MOF.

Good selectivity is vital for the sensor. The influence of potential interfering substances including galactose, fructose, D-sucrose, chitosan, L-cysteine, mannose, glutathione and carbamide on the sensor are recorded. As shown in Fig. 7D and Fig. S8 (Supporting Information), when the concentration of the interfering substance is 10 times higher than the concentration of glucose and other conditions remain unchanged, these potential interfering substances basically have no obvious effect on the absorption value and color of the sensor, indicating the GOD@Cu-hemin MOF can only detect concentration of glucose. The good selectivity just comes from the specific reaction of enzymes. The real sample analysis has been performed by using the GOD@Cu-hemin MOF, showing satisfactory result (Table S3, Supporting Information).

In summary, a novel GOD@Cu-hemin MOF nanocomposite with a ball-flower structure as bienzymatic catalysts for detection of glucose is developed. The as-prepared GOD@Cu-hemin MOF exhibits peroxidase-like activity of Cu-hemin MOF and bioactivity of GOD as well as greatly stability as compared with free HRP and GOD toward harsh conditions such pH and high temperature. The GOD@Cu-hemin MOF is used to construct a glucose sensor and shows a good linear relationship between the glucose concentration range of 10 μM–1.0 mM, and also obtains a lower detection limit of 2.8 μM. At the same time, the prepared colorless transparent agarose gel composite film (Aga/GOD@Cu-hemin MOF/TMB) can be used to colorimetric detection of glucose. The response range is 0.03–0.8 mM with a detection limit of 0.01 mM. The result indicates that the Cu-hemin MOFs are an ideal carrier for the encapsulation of enzyme to fabricate biosensors.

Chunhua Lin: Investigation, Visualization, Writing-Original draft preparation. Yue Du: Investigation, Data curation. Shiqi Wang: Investigation. Li Wang: Writing- review & editing. Yonghai Song: Supervision Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing- review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21465015 and 21665012), and the Open Project Program of Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, Jiangxi Normal University (No. KLFS-KF-201919; KLFS-KF-201913).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2020.111511.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel optical fiber glucose biosensor based on carbon quantum dots-glucose oxidase/cellulose acetate complex sensitive film

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Yu, L.Y. Ding, H.T. Lin, W. Wu, J. Huang, A novel optical fiber glucose biosensor based on carbon quantum dots-glucose oxidase/cellulose acetate complex sensitive film, Biosens. Bioelectron. 146 (2019) 111760.
